verses.commentary: The promise of entering God's rest still stands. Let us be careful not to fall short. The warning is urgent: rest is available, but unbelief forfeits it. Fear here means godly caution, not paralyzing anxiety.

The gospel was preached to them (wilderness Israel) and to us. But the message did not benefit them because they did not combine it with faith. Hearing without believing profits nothing. Faith is the essential response.
We who have believed enter that rest. Belief is the entrance. God's rest has been available since creation finished, yet unbelief bars the door. Rest is not future only; believers enter it now.
Genesis 2:2 cited: On the seventh day God rested from all His works. God's rest is not exhaustion but completion and satisfaction. Creation was finished; God ceased creating and enjoyed what He made.
Yet Psalm 95 says, They shall never enter My rest. This rest still awaits. It is both present (believers enter through faith) and future (full consummation at Christ's return).
Some will enter that rest, but those who first heard the gospel did not enter because of disobedience. Disobedience is the fruit of unbelief. The opportunity remains open for others.
God set another day: Today. Spoken long after Joshua through David in Psalm 95. The opportunity for rest continues. Today is always the moment of decision.
If Joshua had given them rest, God would not have spoken of another day later. Joshua led Israel into Canaan, but that physical rest was not the ultimate rest. Something greater was promised.

There remains a Sabbath rest (sabbatismos) for God's people. Not merely ceasing work, but entering God's own rest—sharing in His completion, satisfaction, and eternal peace. This is eschatological hope.
Anyone who enters God's rest rests from their works, just as God did from His. Not salvation by works, but rest from self-effort. Christ completed the work; we rest in His finished work.

Let us make every effort to enter that rest. The paradox: strive to rest. Effort here means persevering in faith, not earning salvation. We labor to believe, not to achieve. Do not follow Israel's disobedience.

The word of God is living and active. Sharper than any double-edged sword, it penetrates to dividing soul and spirit, joints and marrow. God's word is dynamic, not static. It judges thoughts and attitudes. Nothing is hidden from its penetrating power.

Nothing in creation is hidden from God's sight. Everything is uncovered and laid bare before Him to whom we must give account. Total exposure before omniscient Judge. No secrets, no masks, no evasion.

Since we have a great high priest who has ascended into heaven, Jesus the Son of God, let us hold firmly to the faith we profess. Christ's ascension and priesthood ground our perseverance. He is in heaven interceding for us.

We do not have a high priest unable to sympathize with our weaknesses. He was tempted in every way, just as we are, yet without sin. Jesus knows experientially what temptation feels like. He sympathizes without condescension.

Approach God's throne of grace with confidence to receive mercy and find grace to help in time of need. Boldness (parrēsia) is the privilege of children. We come not as criminals but as beloved children. Mercy forgives our failures; grace empowers our obedience.
